title: Quick example
text:
macroDW <- readRDS(file = here("data/derived-data/macro_dw_raw.rds"))

# clean macros data.frame and aggregate abundances within sites by taxonID
macroDWList = macroDW %>% 
  dplyr::select(siteID, collectDate, sampleID, acceptedTaxonID,sizeClass, dw, no_m2) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(collectDate = as.Date(collectDate)) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(collectMonth = as.Date(
    paste0(
      lubridate::year(collectDate),
      "-",
      lubridate::month(collectDate),
      "-01"),
    format = "%Y-%m-%d"),
    collectYear = lubridate::year(collectDate)) %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(no_m2 = sum(no_m2), .by = c(siteID, collectYear, acceptedTaxonID, sizeClass, dw)) %>% 
  named_group_split(siteID, acceptedTaxonID)

How many groups have only 1 size class?
2097


Here we walk through a procedure to estimate the maximum body size for each taxa using observations across years. For each taxa, we group all observations within a year to best capture the full potential sizes observed. Below is a histogram of all observed body sizes within this example.

## Which has the most observations?
max_r = map_int(macroDWList, ~nrow(.x)) %>% max();max_r

[1] 232

## practice workflow on a single, well-sampled taxa
x = Filter(function(x) nrow(x) == max_r, macroDWList) %>% purrr::flatten_df()

x %>% 
  ggplot()+
  geom_histogram(aes(x = sizeClass, y = ..density.., weight = no_m2))








We then go through a procedure to resample from the size distribution within each year. In this case, the sizes are resampled with replacement to a fixed length (e.g., 1000). We then add variability around the size bin by sampling from a uniform distribution \(\pm\) 0.5mm around the length class.

x_samp = x %>% 
  slice_sample(n = 100, by = collectYear, weight_by = no_m2, replace = TRUE)

x_list = x_samp %>% 
  named_group_split(collectYear) %>% 
  map(~.x %>%
        select(sizeClass) %>%
        mutate(sizeClass_est = midpoint_resample_vec(sizeClass)) %>% 
        select(sizeClass_est) %>% 
        unlist)

From here we fit a suite of models using the size vectors of body sizes. These models are outlined in Heather et al. 2026 “Beyond the single biggest fish: robust inference of species maximum length (\(L_{max}\)) from sample maxima”. That estimate maximum length using Bayesian inference with three different approaches. These have been collected into an R package fishmax.

fishmax::fit_max_model(x_list)

From these models we can look at the estimated maximum lengths with get_max()





model
max_fit.50%
max_lwr.10%
max_upr.90%




EVT (GEV)
40.97927
35.90196
48.73112


EVT (Gumbel)
42.74067
37.61129
49.67276


EFS
38.98511
35.38883
43.81292


EFSmm
52.45721
51.90712
53.00774





and lastly plot the estimates and their uncertainty with plot_max()

title: Data import
text:
This script outlines the process to download macroinvertebrate data from a working database of files released by NEON. The full process for updating this database can be found in the NEONSizeData repository. This process uses the neonstore package to manage data file download and storage for specific NEON data releases that form the underlying derived data products for size analysis. The database of up-to-date files is stored locally on a single computer and the updating of data files is automated. For downstream analysis, the s3 database will serve as the working data source.
We set the specifications of the s3 bucket. This bucket is publicly readable:

# set the s3 bucket specs  
bucket = "neonsizedata-392202703749-us-east-2-an"
prefix = "neonstore/parquet"

We can view the files available on the s3 bucket using the aws.s3 package to view the contents.

get_bucket(bucket = bucket,
           prefix = prefix,
           max = 3)

And we can connect to the s3 bucket to perform actions locally by connecting to the remote bucket:

s3 = arrow::s3_bucket(bucket = paste0(bucket,"/",prefix,"/"))
remote = neon_remote_db(bucket = s3)

and then reading in a file from the remote connection.

neon_remote(
  table = "inv_taxonomyProcessed-basic",
  product = "DP1.20120.001",
  db = remote
  )  %>%
  dplyr::collect() %>% 
  dplyr::glimpse()

title: NEON Invert Max Size
text:
The goal of NEON-invert-max-size is to build a workflow to estimate the maximum body size of macroinvertebrate taxa from stream sites in the National Ecological Observatory Network (NEON).
To do so, we use multiple methods:
1) Extreme Value Theory (EVT)
2) Exact Finite Sampling (EFS)
3) General mass-mortality scaling from a McKendrick-von Foerster transport model
These data are used to explore size-temperature scaling relationships across taxonomic groups and test predictions from macroecological theory on the drivers of body size variation (i.e. Bergmann’s Rule).

title: full-analysis
text:
Introduction

macroDWList <- readRDS(file = here('data/derived-data/macroDWList.rds')) 

# How many sampling years does each taxa-site combination have?
site_taxa_nYear_tab = macroDWList %>% 
  map(~.x %>% 
        dplyr::select(collectYear) %>% 
        distinct %>% 
        unlist %>% 
        length()) %>% 
  bind_rows %>% 
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols= everything(), names_to = 'site-taxa', values_to = 'nYear') 

site_taxa_nYear_tab %>% 
  knitr::kable(., format = 'html', table.attr = "class='dtable'", col.names = c('Site-Taxa', 'nYear'), escape = FALSE)

How many site-taxa combinations have only single year of observations?
2701
How many have 3+ years? 5260

Build a pooled model Negative Binomial count model and test
The most appropriate approach would be to allow us to use the information across many sites when we have many observations. To do so, a hierarchical approach is necessary. Also, there is a fundamental difference between the estimand in the Extreme Value Theory (EVT) models used below and the data we have available. The EVT models are using just the maximum values observed across the sampling years to estimate the largest size – so we are only using something like 3-15 data points. Alternatively, we can try to use all the size information to estimate the maximum body size.
I put together a hierarchical model in stan that uses a negative binomial count model to parameterize the entire body size distribution and then uses posterior predictive simulations to estimate the maximum body size observed using a standardized ‘effort’. This model uses partial-pooling across sites to estimate the body size distribution from a truncated normal distribution so we can use information across many sites to inform poorly sampled sites. The model estimates the expected density or counts for each site along with the mean and standard deviation of the truncated normal. The site-level mean and sd of the truncated normal is estimated with hyperpriors for the pooled mean and sd to allow weighting of sites with lots of data versus sites with few data.
From here, we recognize that the upper body size is something we are unlikely to observe and is very likely dependent on the sampling effort in terms of years of sampling. So to estimate this and characterize the uncertainty, for each draw the model performs 20 posterior predictive simulations and estimates the max body size under a standard ‘effort’.
Below, I test a simulated data set with the negative binomial count model against the models in fishmax. I deliberately choose a low value for the site with 1 sample to determine the effect of the pooling. This allows us to see that the model is performing as expected and estimating reasonable values. I also compare the maximum observed body size from sampling 100,000 individuals from the distributions to see expected values with extensive sampling of a distribution


## set the simulation parameters
simPars = data.frame(site = c('a','b','c','d','e'),
                     mu = c(10,8,7.8, 3, 9.5),
                     sigma = c(1.0,1.2,1.5,0.5,1.8),
                     k = c(4,3,3,1,2),
                     stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
                     
simPars$n = list(c(100,150,200,110),
                           c(100,200,210),
                           c(500,600,700),
                           c(50),
                           c(200,220))

# show histograms of body size distributions for 10,000 individuals
simList = mapply(FUN = truncnorm::rtruncnorm,mean = c(10,8,7.8, 3, 9.5),sd = c(1.0,1.2,1.5,0.5,1.8),
                 MoreArgs = list(n = 1e5,
                                 a = 0))
# plot the distributions
par(mfrow = c(2,3))
apply(simList,2,hist)

## simulate the sampling process and spit out in stan_data format
sim = simulation(simPars = simPars, seed = 1312)

# aggregate to test the simulated observations
# add the max values observed from 10,000 inds for a sanity check

simAgg = do.call(
  data.frame,
  aggregate(
    x ~ site,
    data = sim$observations,
    FUN = function(z) {
      c(
        mean = mean(z),
        sd = sd(z),
        min = min(z),
        max = max(z)
        )
    }
    )
  ) %>% 
  bind_cols(
    data.frame(
    x.mean.1e5 = apply(simList,2,mean),
    x.sd.1e5 = apply(simList,2,sd),
    x.max.1e5 = apply(simList,2,max)
    )
  ) 

simAgg %>% 
  knitr::kable(., caption = "Table 1. Comparisons of distribution moments from simulated sampling data and 1e5 draws from truncated normal.")

The x.max from the 100,000 individual simulations have a slightly larger max as expected but everything is pretty close, as expected.


## pull out the stan_data from sim and add the reference sample #
stan_data = sim$stan_data
stan_data$k_ref = 20L
stan_data$mu_upper <- 200
stan_data$sigma_upper <- 100
stan_data$prior_only = 0

# compile stan model
# mod = cmdstan_model(here('code/stan/neg-bin_count_pooled.stan')) #old nb model before stabilizing priors and initialization values to help sampling
mod = cmdstan_model(here('code/stan/hierarchical_nb_max_size_stable.stan'))

init_list= make_init_list_stable(stan_data, chains = 4, seed = 1312)
# fit the model
negbin_fit = mod$sample(
    data = stan_data,
    seed = 1312,
    chains = 4,
    parallel_chains = 4,
    init = init_list,
    iter_warmup = 1000,
    iter_sampling = 1000,
    adapt_delta = 0.95,
    max_treedepth = 12,
    refresh = 0
  )

  setNames(., nm = c('maxL_mean','maxL_sd','maxL_q5','maxL_q95'))

bind_cols(sim$site_truth[,c(1:2,5:6)], slimSumm) %>% 
  bind_cols(
    data.frame(

    )

'site' and 'site_id' represent the site identifiers, 'k_observed' is the number of samples, 'mean_n_observed' is the average individuals in each samples, 'maxL_*' represents the modeled estimates of the maximum body size, and 'maxL_1e5' is the maximum body size observed in 1e5 simulations from truncated normal distribution.")

We can’t fit a model with fishmax to site ‘d’ because we only have a single sample, but we had no problem with the hierarchical model to get an estimate for this site.

title: full-analysis
text:
Prepare NEON data for analysis

# list organized by each taxa group, acceptedTaxonID
macroTaxaList = readRDS(here('data/derived-data/macroTaxaList.rds'))

# get rough mean sizes quickly to set priors on upper mu bound
means = macroTaxaList %>% 
  map(~.x %>% select(sizeClass) %>% unlist %>%  mean(., na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
  bind_rows %>% unlist
hist(log10(means))
mean(log(means));mean(means);max(means)
# [1] 1.323187
# [1] 5.177346
# [1] 115
sds = macroTaxaList %>% 
  map(~.x %>% select(sizeClass) %>% unlist %>%  sd(., na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
  bind_rows %>% unlist
hist(log10(sds))
mean(sds, na.rm = TRUE);max(sds, na.rm = TRUE)
#[1] 2.194067
#[1] 26.71454
# we have some means above 100
# we set mu_upper to 400 and sigma_upper to 200

# convert the taxa sample lists in the correct format for the negative binomial model
macroTaxaList %>% 
  future_map(~make_stanData_taxa(.x),
             .options = furrr_options(seed= 1312)
      ) %>% 
  # this is suprisingly a large file. saved to ignore/ folder.
saveRDS(here('ignore/macroTaxaStanData.rds'))

# make a site index list to map back to model output to sites
macroTaxaList %>% 
  future_map(~make_site_index(.x),
              .options = furrr_options(seed= 1312)) %>% 
  saveRDS(., here('ignore/macroTaxaSiteIndex.rds'))


# set a future plan for multisession fitting if one isn't already set
if(!inherits(plan(), "multisession")){
  if(parallelly::availableCores() == 24)
    future::plan("multisession", workers = 10, gc = TRUE)
  } else{
    future::plan("multisession", workers = 6, gc = TRUE)
}

# compile stan models
## model for multi-site taxa
mod = cmdstan_model(here('code/stan/hierarchical_nb_max_size_stable.stan'))
## model for taxa with only observations at a single site
single_mod = cmdstan_model(here('code/stan/single_nb_max_size_stable.stan'))

## load in stan data list
macroTaxaStanData = readRDS(here('ignore/macroTaxaStanData.rds')) 

# reorder macroTaxaStanData by most rows to fewest for distributing future work
macroTaxaStanData = macroTaxaStanData[order(sapply(macroTaxaStanData, function(x) x$n_obs), decreasing = TRUE)]
## set the taxa names for naming models
taxaNames = names(macroTaxaStanData)

## based on the upper bounds of the initialization values, we set an upper bound of 4.5 for the cross site count variation. This corresponds to ~55x variation in counts among sites at 1SD.
## posterior checks will need to be done on this variable to ensure we are not getting a lot 
## of mass in this upper bound and also some prior sensitivity check to ensure we are not 
## massively impacting inferences with this very large intersite-count difference prior.
### run tests with one model to test overwrite
# stan_data = macroTaxaStanData[["CHISP8"]]
# y = "CHISP8"
# debugonce(fit_negbin_named)
# fit_negbin_named(stan_data = stan_data, taxaName = y, rerun = TRUE, overwrite = FALSE)
# z = CAESP5_negbin$summary()

# fit the models. This will overwrite existing models if overwrite = TRUE. These are saved in the ignore/ folder
# future_walk2(
#   macroTaxaStanData,
#   taxaNames,
#   ~fit_negbin_named(stan_data = .x, taxaName = .y, rerun = TRUE, overwrite = FALSE),
#   .options = furrr_options(seed = 1312, scheduling = Inf)
#   )
## a non-future version for debugging model fits
# walk2(
#    macroTaxaStanData,
#    taxaNames,
#    ~fit_negbin_named(stan_data  = .x, taxaName = .y, rerun = TRUE, overwrite = FALSE)
#    )


Note of caution
Some of the checking of model behavior is outlined in the model-troubleshooting file to keep this clean. The majority of this was dealing with divergences when trying to fit a hiearchical model to single-site data (duh!), but a few others need to be checked to ensure good inferences. These results below are preliminary and once updated this warning will be removed.


Checking model results

# load in the model files lists
nbModFiles = list.files(here('ignore/models/'), '.rds', full.names = TRUE)

# extract taxa names from model lists
taxaNames = nbModFiles %>% 
  map(~sapply(strsplit(sapply(strsplit(.x, "/"),"[",10),"_"), "[", 1)) %>% 
  unlist

# load in site index for each taxa
macroTaxaSiteIndex = readRDS(here('ignore/macroTaxaSiteIndex.rds'))
# set the model index order to each taxa
macroTaxaSiteIndex = macroTaxaSiteIndex[taxaNames]

## read in the taxonomy data to join with index
macroTaxonomy = readRDS(here('data/NEON-macros-tax.rds'))

# read in length-mass regression  
lwCoefsDf = read.csv(here('data/macro_lw_coeffs.csv')) #%>% 
  # add in others that don't have l-m below

# add this to the macroTaxonomy to read in later.
# debugonce(LW_coef)
macroTaxonomy = macroTaxonomy %>% 
  LW_coef(., lw_coef = lwCoefsDf, percent = TRUE)
# read in and clean the site lat-long data to merge with index
siteCoords = read.csv(here('data/site_latlong.csv')) %>% 
  bind_rows(data.frame(
    site = c('BLWA', 'FLNT', 'LIRO','PRLA','SUGG','BARC','PRPO','CRAM','TOOK','TOMB'),
    lat = c(32.54153,31.18542,45.99827,47.15909,29.68778,29.67598,47.12984,46.20967,68.63069,31.85343),
    long = c(-87.79815,-84.43740,-89.70477,-99.11388,-82.01775,-82.00841,-99.25315,-89.47369,-149.61064,-88.15887)
  ))
  
# here is the function to prepare the full data frame with estimated maximum mass. This saves the table so we don't have rerun this every time. Takes 20min (too long) to run sequentially.
if(rerun_data){
  if(!inherits('multisession', plan())){
    future::plan('multisession', workers = 10, gc = TRUE)
  }
future_pmap(
  list(nbModFiles, taxaNames, macroTaxaSiteIndex),
  ~extract_max(filePath = ..1, names = ..2, siteIndex = ..3)
  ) %>%
  bind_rows() %>% 
  dplyr::select(-siteID) %>% 
  dplyr::select(acceptedTaxonID, site = siteName, everything()) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(site = trimws(as.character(site))) %>% 
  right_join(macroTaxonomy, ., by = 'acceptedTaxonID') %>% 
  left_join(., siteCoords, by = 'site') %>% 
  saveRDS(here('data/derived-data/macroTaxaSiteDf.rds'))
} else{
  macroTaxaSiteDf = readRDS(here('data/derived-data/macroTaxaSiteDf.rds'))
}

We can extract all the model estimates and merge with site and taxonomy data to look at patterns.

# use the est_dw() function to convert length estimates to mass 
macroTaxaSiteDf =readRDS(here('data/derived-data/macroTaxaSiteDf.rds')) %>%
  mutate(across(c(mean, median, sd, q5, q95),
                ~est_dw_simp(.x, a,b,formula_type),
                .names = "dw_{.col}")) %>% 
  ## do some fixes of taxa with missing information
  dplyr::mutate(genus = case_when(scientificName == 'Monophylax mono' ~ 'Monophylax',
                                  scientificName == 'Nanocladius (Plecopterocoluthus) sp.' ~ 'Nanocladius',
                                  .default = genus)) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(lowestClassification = case_when(
    stringr::str_detect(scientificName,'nae') & is.na(genus) & family %in% 'Chironomidae' ~ 'subfamily',
    stringr::str_detect(scientificName, 'dae', negate = TRUE) & is.na(genus) & family %in% 'Chironomidae' ~ 'tribe',
    is.na(genus) & family %in% 'Chironomidae' ~ 'family',
    is.na(subclass) & is.na(order) & is.na(family) & is.na(genus)~ 'class',
    is.na(order) & is.na(family) & is.na(genus) ~ 'subclass',
    is.na(family) & is.na(genus) ~ 'order',
    is.na(genus) ~ 'family',
    specificEpithet %in% c('sp.','gr.') ~ 'genus',
    .default = 'species')) %>% 
  dplyr::select(scientificName, acceptedTaxonID, class, subclass, order, family, genus, specificEpithet, lowestClassification, site, lat, long, variable, dw_mean, dw_median, dw_sd, dw_q5, dw_q95)

Now we have all the estimated variables we might want to model and we have converted from length to mass, let’s take a look at some summaries of the distinct counts at different taxonomic levels.

classTab = macroTaxaSiteDf %>% 
  dplyr::filter(grepl('size_mean',variable),
                !is.na(class)) %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(n = n(), .by = 'class')

subclassTab = macroTaxaSiteDf %>% 
  dplyr::filter(grepl('size_mean',variable),
                !is.na(subclass)) %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(n = n(), .by = 'subclass')
  
orderTab = macroTaxaSiteDf %>% 
  dplyr::filter(grepl('size_mean',variable),
                !is.na(order)) %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(n = n(), .by = 'order')
  
familyTab = macroTaxaSiteDf %>% 
  dplyr::filter(grepl('size_mean',variable),
                !is.na(family)) %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(n = n(), .by = 'family')

genusTab = macroTaxaSiteDf %>% 
  dplyr::filter(grepl('size_mean',variable),
                !is.na(genus)) %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(n = n(), .by = 'genus')

taxonIDTab = macroTaxaSiteDf %>% 
  dplyr::filter(grepl('size_mean', variable),
                !is.na(acceptedTaxonID)) %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(n = n(), .by = 'acceptedTaxonID')

title: full-analysis
text:
Fitting max length models
For the site-taxa combinations with 3+ years of observations we can use the fishmax package to estimate body size. For the other groups, we will want to devise another approach to get estimates.

#### This subsets samples to those with 3+ years and creates a list for 
#### modeling. This is a large file and is held only locally currently. 
#### If running this analysis, this will have to be recreated

# find the taxa with only 3+ years
nYear_3 = which(site_taxa_nYear_tab$nYear >= 3)
# select the taxa with only 3+ years
macroDWList_nYear_3 = macroDWList[nYear_3]

# resample the sizes to reduce file size.
# This is not ideal. We want to be able to use all the data if possible.
set.seed(1312)
macroDWList_nYear_3_samps = macroDWList_nYear_3 %>% 
  map(~slice_sample(.x, n = 500, by = collectYear, weight_by = no_m2, replace = TRUE))

macroDWList_nYear_3_list = macroDWList_nYear_3_samps %>% 
  map(~.x %>% 
  named_group_split(collectYear) %>% 
  map(~.x %>%
        select(sizeClass) %>%
        mutate(sizeClass_est = midpoint_resample_vec(sizeClass)) %>% 
        select(sizeClass_est) %>% 
        unlist))

# place the large file in the ignore folder. 
saveRDS(macroDWList_nYear_3_list, here('ignore/macroDWList_nYear_3_list.rds'))

To run this on all ~5200 taxa-site observations takes ~10 hours. We could modify the code to estimate body sizes for each taxon in a hierarchical model that uses total information from all sites & years to estimate body sizes in sites where we only have a single years’ observation using partial pooling across all samples.

# load in the large 3+ years list
macroDWList_nYear_3_list = readRDS(here('ignore/macroDWList_nYear_3_list.rds'))
# set the model names for each site-taxa
mod_names = lapply(names(macroDWList_nYear_3_list), FUN = function(a) gsub("^(\\w+)\\s\\/\\s(\\w+)$", "\\2_\\1",a))

# test the named fit_max_model function
 # x = macroDWList_nYear_3_list[[1]]
 # y = unlist(mod_names[1])
 # debugonce(fit_max_model_named)
 # z =  fit_max_model_named(df = x, mod_name = y, rerun = TRUE, overwrite = TRUE, model_types = 'evt')

# set the multisession options for parallel work
future::plan("multisession", workers = 10)

# walk through each taxa and fit multi gvt models in fishmax
# these models are saved to the ignore/models/ folder
# fitting all ~5200 models with the above multisession plan took 
# ~ 10 hours to complete
furrr::future_walk2(
   macroDWList_nYear_3_list,
   mod_names,
   ~fit_max_model_named(df = .x, mod_name = .y, rerun = TRUE, overwrite = FALSE, model_types = c('gvt','gvt_gumbel')),
   .options = furrr_options(
     seed = 1312
     )
   )

We can then extract the sizes

modPaths = list.files(here('ignore/models/'), pattern ='.rds', full.names = TRUE)

## test the get_max wide function
# debugonce(get_max_wide)
# x = readRDS(file = modPaths[1])
# get_max_wide(mod_path = modPaths[1])

## run all the models and save to a file for later
modPaths %>% furrr::future_map(get_max_wide) %>% bind_rows() %>% saveRDS(., here('data/max_size.rds'))

title: model-troubleshooting
text:
$system
sequential:
- args: function (..., envir = parent.frame(), workers = "<NULL>")
- tweaked: FALSE
- call: NULL


This page is for documenting the model checking procedures for the maximum size estimation models. The models were all fit originally using the hierarchical negative binomial model code/stan/hierarchical_nb_max_size_stable. With these fits, we identified a number of issues, so we are now working backwards to simpler models. I outline below the steps to keep a record for future.

title: model-troubleshooting
text:
$system
sequential:
- args: function (..., envir = parent.frame(), workers = "<NULL>")
- tweaked: FALSE
- call: NULL


This page is for documenting the model checking procedures for the maximum size estimation models. The models were all fit originally using the hierarchical negative binomial model code/stan/hierarchical_nb_max_size_stable. With these fits, we identified a number of issues, so we are now working backwards to simpler models. I outline below the steps to keep a record for future.

title: model-troubleshooting
text:
Divergent transitions
The original models had ~700, or about 50%, that exhibited divergent transitions after warmup. I should have realized at the outset that we are likely to have many taxa with only a single site, so the hierarchical structure of the model is obviously not appropriate. It turned out that ~400 only have a single site.

macroTaxaStanData = readRDS(here('ignore/macroTaxaStanData.rds')) 

macroTaxaStanData %>% 
  map_lgl(~.x$S == 1) %>% sum

This is a large part of the issue. Which became apparent with the checks below.

Check models with divergences after sampling
Lots of the models exhibit divergences after warming, most often this is ~1% of transitions, other times if can be up to ~20%. Below we go through a diagnostic procedure to better characterize where these divergences occur and hopefully we can better specify/constrain the model to eliminate this issue.

nbFiles = list.files(here('ignore/models/'), '.rds', full.names = TRUE)
taxaNames = nbFiles %>% 
  map(~sapply(strsplit(sapply(strsplit(.x, "/"),"[",10),"_"), "[", 1)) %>% 
  unlist
nbFiles = setNames(nbFiles, taxaNames)
# check for # of divergences
nbDivergences = nbFiles %>% 
  future_map(~check_divergences(filePath = .x))
# what is the distribution of divergent #s
nbDivergences %>% map(~.x %>% pluck('num_diverge') %>% unlist) %>% unlist %>% hist
# what is maximum # of divergences
nbDivergences %>% map(~.x %>% pluck('num_diverge') %>% unlist) %>% unlist %>% max
which(nbDivergences %>% map(~.x %>% pluck('num_diverge') %>% unlist) %>% unlist == 1116)
#what is the 75-percentile # of divergences
nbDivergences %>% map(~.x %>% pluck('num_diverge') %>% unlist) %>% unlist %>% quantile(., 0.75)


# separate out the models with divergences
nbDivergentFiles = nbFiles[nbDivergences %>% map(~pluck(.x, 'diverge')) %>% unlist]
# check with a particularly problematic model
highDiverge = nbDivergences %>% 
  map(~.x %>% pluck('num_diverge') %>% unlist) %>% 
  unlist 


divergeStanData = macroTaxaStanData[names(macroTaxaStanData) %in% names(nbDivergentFiles )]

# how many of the divergence taxa have only a single site?
divergeStanData %>% 
  map(~.x$S) %>% 
  unlist %>%
  keep(~.x == 1) %>% 
  count()
# all sites with only 1 site show divergences

# how many of the divergence taxa have only 2 samples?
divergeStanData %>% 
  map(~.x$K) %>% 
  unlist %>% 
  keep(~.x == 2) %>% 
  count()

To address this issue, we built a simpler single site model as that is the most straightforward way to address this issue. This model is code/stan/single_nb_max_size_stable.stan. So with this new approach, I refit just the taxa with observations at only a single site.

# do a clean load of the stan data to ensure we don't mess anything up
## I am only loading the single model here to ensure we don't accidentally overwrite a model we don't want to 
single_mod = cmdstan_model(here('code/stan/single_nb_max_size_stable.stan'))

macroTaxaStanData = readRDS(here('ignore/macroTaxaStanData.rds')) 
# reorder macroTaxaStanData by most rows to fewest
macroTaxaStanData = macroTaxaStanData[order(sapply(macroTaxaStanData, function(x) x$n_obs), decreasing = TRUE)]
taxaNames = names(macroTaxaStanData)

# first identify the locations of the data with only one site
## convert to logical vector for subsetting stan data and names
macroTaxaSingleVec = macroTaxaStanData %>% 
  map_lgl(~.x$S == 1) %>% unlist 

macroTaxaSingleStanData = macroTaxaStanData[macroTaxaSingleVec]
singleTaxaNames = taxaNames[macroTaxaSingleVec]

# model worked with a single taxa (on only two observations)
# model worked with a single taxa on 22 observations

# ran into an issue that interrupted the future session after many files were written
modFiles = list.files(here('ignore/models/'), '.rds', full.names = TRUE)
fileMeta = file.info(modFiles)
targetTime = as.POSIXct("2026-07-22 17:54:00 CDT")
recentFiles = modFiles[fileMeta$mtime > targetTime]

taxaRecent = singleTaxaNames %>% 
  map_lgl(~grepl(.x, recentFiles, ignore.case = TRUE) %>% sum %>% as.logical)

x = macroTaxaSingleStanData[!taxaRecent]
y = singleTaxaNames[!taxaRecent]
# debugonce(fit_negbin_named)
future_walk2(
   x,
   y,
   ~fit_negbin_named(stan_data  = .x, taxaName = .y, rerun = TRUE, overwrite = TRUE),
  .options = furrr_options(seed = 1312, scheduling = Inf)
   )
# one of the models 3 chains failed need to find this somehow :(

A few models still have divergences. I will troubleshoot these but I wanted to work on importing and passing along initial results while I work on divergences.

# make a list of initialization values for checking 
if(rerun_data){
  init_lists = macroTaxaStanData %>% 
  map(~\(x){
    if(x$S == 1){
      make_init_list_single_site(
        stan_data = .x,
        chains = 4L,
        seed = 1312
      )
    } else{
      make_init_list_stable(
        stan_data = .x,
        chains = 4L,
        seed = 1312)
    }
    }
    ) %>%
    saveRDS(here('ignore/nb_init_lists.rds'))
} else{
  init_lists = readRDS(here('ignore/nb_init_lists.rds'))
}
# code to check initialization values
tau_log_lambda_inits = init_lists %>% 
  future_map(
    ~vapply(.x, function(x) x$tau_log_lambda, numeric(1))
  ) %>% 
  bind_rows(.id = 'taxaName') %>% 
  t() %>% data.frame %>% 
  tibble::rownames_to_column('taxaName') %>% 
  mutate(max = pmax(X1,X2,X3,X4, na.rm = TRUE))
hist(tau_log_lambda_inits$max)


fit = readRDS(x)

diagnostics <- fit$diagnostic_summary(
  diagnostics = c(
    "divergences",
    "treedepth",
    "ebfmi"
  ),
  quiet = TRUE
)

diagnostics
# a single chain is the main issue here
sampler_diag <- fit$sampler_diagnostics(
  format = "df"
)

divergence_rate <- mean(
  sampler_diag$divergent__
)#17% of transitions had divergences

parameter_names <- posterior::variables(
  fit$draws()
)

key_parameters <- grep(
  paste0(
    "^(",
    paste(
      c(
        "alpha_log_mu",
        "alpha_log_sigma",
        "tau_log_mu",
        "tau_log_sigma",
        "log_mu_site\\[",
        "log_sigma_site\\[",
        "alpha_log_lambda",
        "tau_log_lambda",
        "z_lambda\\[",
        "log_lambda\\[",
        "log_phi",
        "phi",
        "mu\\[",
        "sigma\\["
      ),
      collapse = "|"
    ),
    ")"
  ),
  parameter_names,
  value = TRUE
)

key_parameters

get_divergence_draws <- function(
    fit,
    variables
) {
  draws <- as.data.frame(
    fit$draws(
      variables = variables,
      format = "df"
    )
  )

  diagnostics <- as.data.frame(
    fit$sampler_diagnostics(
      format = "df"
    )
  )

  draw_key <- paste(
    draws$.chain,
    draws$.iteration,
    sep = ":"
  )

  diagnostic_key <- paste(
    diagnostics$.chain,
    diagnostics$.iteration,
    sep = ":"
  )

  diagnostic_row <- match(
    draw_key,
    diagnostic_key
  )

  if (anyNA(diagnostic_row)) {
    stop(
      "Could not match all posterior draws ",
      "to sampler diagnostics."
    )
  }

  draws$divergent__ <-
    diagnostics$divergent__[diagnostic_row]

  draws$energy__ <-
    diagnostics$energy__[diagnostic_row]

  draws$accept_stat__ <-
    diagnostics$accept_stat__[diagnostic_row]

  draws$treedepth__ <-
    diagnostics$treedepth__[diagnostic_row]

  draws
}

divergence_draws <- get_divergence_draws(
  fit,
  key_parameters
)

divergent_draws_df <- divergence_draws[
  divergence_draws$divergent__ == 1,
]

nondivergent_draws_df <- divergence_draws[
  divergence_draws$divergent__ == 0,
]

screen_divergent_parameters <- function(
    fit,
    variables
) {
  dat <- get_divergence_draws(
    fit = fit,
    variables = variables
  )

  is_divergent <- dat$divergent__ == 1

  if (!any(is_divergent)) {
    return(
      data.frame(
        variable = variables,
        n_divergent = 0L,
        ks_distance = NA_real_,
        median_shift_mad = NA_real_,
        divergent_rank_median = NA_real_,
        divergent_rank_iqr = NA_real_
      )
    )
  }

  results <- lapply(
    variables,
    function(variable) {
      x <- dat[[variable]]

      keep <- is.finite(x)

      x <- x[keep]
      div <- is_divergent[keep]

      x_div <- x[div]
      x_ok <- x[!div]

      if (
        length(x_div) < 2L ||
        length(x_ok) < 2L
      ) {
        return(
          data.frame(
            variable = variable,
            n_divergent = length(x_div),
            ks_distance = NA_real_,
            median_shift_mad = NA_real_,
            divergent_rank_median = NA_real_,
            divergent_rank_iqr = NA_real_
          )
        )
      }

      parameter_scale <- stats::mad(
        x,
        constant = 1.4826
      )

      if (
        !is.finite(parameter_scale) ||
        parameter_scale <= 0
      ) {
        parameter_scale <- stats::sd(x)
      }

      if (
        !is.finite(parameter_scale) ||
        parameter_scale <= 0
      ) {
        parameter_scale <- 1
      }

      ks_result <- suppressWarnings(
        stats::ks.test(
          x_div,
          x_ok,
          exact = FALSE
        )
      )

      scaled_ranks <-
        rank(
          x,
          ties.method = "average"
        ) / (length(x) + 1)

      div_ranks <- scaled_ranks[div]

      data.frame(
        variable = variable,
        n_divergent = length(x_div),

        median_divergent =
          stats::median(x_div),

        median_nondivergent =
          stats::median(x_ok),

        ks_distance =
          unname(ks_result$statistic),

        median_shift_mad =
          abs(
            stats::median(x_div) -
              stats::median(x_ok)
          ) / parameter_scale,

        divergent_rank_median =
          stats::median(div_ranks),

        divergent_rank_iqr =
          stats::IQR(div_ranks)
      )
    }
  )

  results <- do.call(
    rbind,
    results
  )

  results[
    order(
      results$ks_distance,
      decreasing = TRUE,
      na.last = TRUE
    ),
  ]
}

divergence_screen <- screen_divergent_parameters(
  fit,
  key_parameters
)

head(
  divergence_screen,
  20
)

make_bayesplot_nuts <- function(fit) {
  diagnostics <- as.data.frame(
    fit$sampler_diagnostics(
      format = "df"
    )
  )

  diagnostic_variables <- setdiff(
    names(diagnostics),
    c(
      ".chain",
      ".iteration",
      ".draw"
    )
  )

  long_list <- lapply(
    diagnostic_variables,
    function(variable) {
      data.frame(
        Chain = diagnostics$.chain,
        Iteration = diagnostics$.iteration,
        Parameter = variable,
        Value = diagnostics[[variable]]
      )
    }
  )

  do.call(
    rbind,
    long_list
  )
}

nuts_parameters <- make_bayesplot_nuts(
  fit
)

top_parameters <- head(
  divergence_screen$variable,
  6
)

top_draws <- fit$draws(

  format = "array"
)

bayesplot::mcmc_pairs(
  top_draws,
  pars = top_parameters,
  np = nuts_parameters,
  condition = bayesplot::pairs_condition(
    nuts = "divergent__"
  ),
  off_diag_args = list(
    alpha = 0.35,
    size = 0.6
  ),
  grid_args = list(
    top = "Divergent versus nondivergent draws"
  )
)

bayesplot::mcmc_parcoord(
  top_draws,
  pars = top_parameters,
  transformations = function(x) {
    scale_value <- stats::mad(
      x,
      constant = 1.4826
    )

    if (
      !is.finite(scale_value) ||
      scale_value <= 0
    ) {
      scale_value <- stats::sd(x)
    }

    if (
      !is.finite(scale_value) ||
      scale_value <= 0
    ) {
      return(x - stats::median(x))
    }

    (
      x - stats::median(x)
    ) / scale_value
  },
  np = nuts_parameters
)

bayesplot::mcmc_trace(
  top_draws,
  pars = top_parameters,
  np = nuts_parameters
)

Bad models
Some of the models just have too few sites/samples to adequately and reasonably fit the model. We can prescreen these models using some minimum criteria such as # of sampling events. Below we explore the data and sampling space that we show divergences.

badTaxa = c(
  "DRUCOR",
)



Check posteriors for some taxa with initializations near boundary
Some of the initial values for the tau_log_lambda parameters run up against the set boundaries for this parameters. The value is set at an upper range of 4. This parameter characterizes the across site expectation in log expected counts. We bounded this to avoid drawing prior draws that lead to Inf values during sampling. However, if counts vary greatly among sites, our initial values might exceed this limit leading to rejection of some or all chains. For context, this parameter represents a multiplicative difference in counts, therefore with a limit of 4, as set, corresponds to a 55x difference in counts for a site that is one-standard deviation. I include a step to check the initialization values for better model specification in the future.
